name: Generating Functions — Sequences as Polynomials
whatIsIt: A GENERATING FUNCTION packs a whole sequence a₀, a₁, a₂, … into ONE power series A(x) = a₀ + a₁x + a₂x² + a₃x³ + …. The sequence lives in the COEFFICIENTS. Key closed forms: 1/(1−x) = 1 + x + x² + x³ + … (all ones), and 1/(1−x)² = 1 + 2x + 3x² + 4x³ + … (the coefficient of xⁿ is n+1). Multiplying two generating functions ADDS up all the ways to split a count — turning counting problems into algebra.
whyNeeded: Hard counting and recurrence problems become routine algebra once the sequence is a function: you manipulate one expression instead of chasing terms. It's a signature advanced-Olympiad and combinatorics tool.
realLifeUses: Counting the ways to make change with given coin values
Solving recurrences like Fibonacci in closed form
Counting distributions of identical items into boxes
Probability of sums when rolling several dice

vocabulary.meaning: A power series whose coefficients are the sequence terms.
The nth term of the encoded sequence.
A compact expression (like 1/(1−x)) for an infinite series.

story.title: The Sequence in Disguise
story.text: Ishan meets the series 1 + x + x² + x³ + … . 'That's just 1/(1−x),' says the Professor. 'The sequence 1, 1, 1, 1, … is hiding in the coefficients.' Then he squares it: 1/(1−x)² = 1 + 2x + 3x² + 4x³ + … . 'Now the coefficients are 1, 2, 3, 4 — the counting numbers! Each coefficient of xⁿ is n+1.' Ishan grins: 'One little function stores an entire sequence.'
story.extractedProblem: In 1/(1−x)² = 1 + 2x + 3x² + 4x³ + …, what is the coefficient of x³?
story.answerInStory: 4 — the coefficient of xⁿ in 1/(1−x)² is n+1, so for n = 3 it is 4.

visual.caption: A generating function stores a sequence in its coefficients. Here 1/(1−x)² holds 1, 2, 3, 4 … — the coefficient of xⁿ is n+1.

standardMethod.summary: Encode the sequence as A(x) = Σ aₙxⁿ. Replace it with a closed form using known series (1/(1−x), 1/(1−x)², etc.). Manipulate algebraically, then read the coefficient of xⁿ to recover the term you want.
steps: Write the sequence as coefficients of a power series.
Swap in a closed form such as 1/(1−x) = Σ xⁿ.
Extract the coefficient of xⁿ — that's your answer.

workedExamples.problem: In 1/(1−x) = 1 + x + x² + x³ + …, find the coefficient of x³.
steps: So the coefficient of x³ is 1.

workedExamples.problem: In 1/(1−x)² = 1 + 2x + 3x² + 4x³ + …, find the coefficient of x³.

commonMistakes.mistake: Plugging in a number for x instead of reading a coefficient.
commonMistakes.fix: The sequence lives in the COEFFICIENTS of xⁿ, not in the function's value at some x.

commonMistakes.mistake: Taking the coefficient of xⁿ in 1/(1−x)² as n.
commonMistakes.fix: It is n + 1: 1/(1−x)² = 1 + 2x + 3x² + …, so xⁿ has coefficient n + 1.

questions.q: The sequence encoded by a generating function lives in the…
In 1/(1−x)², the coefficient of xⁿ is…

teachBackPrompt: Show a grown-up that 1 + x + x² + x³ + … = 1/(1−x) stores the sequence 1,1,1,1…, and that squaring it gives 1/(1−x)² = 1 + 2x + 3x² + 4x³ …, storing 1,2,3,4. Explain that the answer is always the coefficient of xⁿ.

revisionCard.summary: Generating function: A(x) = Σ aₙxⁿ stores a sequence in its coefficients. Key forms: 1/(1−x) = Σ xⁿ (all ones); 1/(1−x)² = Σ(n+1)xⁿ. Multiplying generating functions convolves (adds up splits). Answer = coefficient of xⁿ, not the value.

rememberIt.hook: Pack a sequence into coefficients: 1/(1−x) = Σxⁿ
rememberIt.unpack: 1/(1−x)² = Σ(n+1)xⁿ. Multiply GFs to count combined choices. Read the xⁿ coefficient.

studentQuestions.q: Why does multiplying generating functions count things?
studentQuestions.a: The coefficient of xⁿ in a product sums a₀bₙ + a₁bₙ₋₁ + … — exactly the number of ways to split n between the two parts.

studentQuestions.q: How does this solve a recurrence?
studentQuestions.a: Turn the recurrence into an equation for A(x), solve algebraically, then expand back into coefficients to get a closed form for each term.

formulas.name: Geometric GF
formulas.whenToUse: Encoding a constant sequence / base tool.
